All reports
Reverse Cavity
REVERSE CAVITY

Voice of Customer Report

What customers actually say, and the language that moves them.

Prepared by [Author - to be filled]
June 2026

0. Executive Summary

Bottom line. Customers in this category come for three things in their own words: to get a dentist to confirm the cavities are gone, to avoid invasive and expensive dental work they distrust, and to feel their teeth get stronger fast. This report is built entirely from public sources, because no first-party Reverse Cavity customers were interviewed in this engagement; the single highest-value next step is to commission a primary-interview pass to confirm what the reviews suggest.

Customers want three things, and they say them plainly.

They want a dentist to confirm the result. Pedro’s whole story turns on the second visit: “After 3 or 4 weeks I went to the dentist and she [the dentist] didn’t find anything.”1 The proof customers crave is not the brand’s word, it is their own dentist’s.

They want out of the dental system they distrust and cannot afford. Thekla McDaniels names the fear in one image: “an implant costs as much as a decent used car)…. Mooo, maybe I am now a cash cow for them!”7

They want fast, felt strength. Nathaniel measured it in days: “about ten days in of strictly following it there was an overall increased feeling of strength in my teeth.”3

The biggest gap between how this category talks and how customers talk: vendors lead with mechanism (remineralize, works from the inside out), and customers lead with relief, fear, money, and their dentist’s verdict. The single highest-value language shift is to stop selling the chemistry and start selling the dentist visit that confirms it.

This report draws on public reviews and testimonials only. No Reverse Cavity customer was interviewed. Each pattern below traces to a cited public source, and a primary-interview program would sharpen them into segmented messaging the company can act on.

0.5 Evidence Base and Recommended Primary Research

Every quote in this report comes from a public source: Reverse Cavity’s own brand sites (reversecavity.com and the Reverse Autoimmune protocol-kit landing page), the founder’s published before-and-after article, and four outside platforms where this category’s buyers talk openly. The outside platforms are Thingtesting (Boka hydroxyapatite reviews), the Permies forum (a long ‘Tooth remineralization quandary’ thread), Zellie’s product reviews, and Living Well with Dr. Michelle Jorgensen. The two interviews conducted for this engagement were both with company leadership: the founder, Gabriel Arruda, and the brand strategist. No Reverse Cavity customer was interviewed first-hand.

That matters for how the findings should be read. Brand-site testimonials are curated and tell a consistent winning story. The outside forums and review pages carry the unflattering material the brand surfaces never show: the regret, the defections, the buyers who never fit the founder’s profile at all. The honest read is that the brand sites confirm the founder’s intended customer, and the outside sources reveal a wider and more divided market around it.

Commission a primary-interview and case-study program as the next step, run as AI-led custom interviews with Reverse Cavity’s own buyers and a set of lapsed ones. It would confirm or sharpen four things the public record can only suggest: the exact emotional registers that drive purchase, the split between the person who pays and the person who uses the protocol, the timeline from first thought to repeat purchase, and the forces that win or lose the sale. The brand already holds the raw material. The founder said he has collected roughly two dozen written testimonials and seven video ones; those customers are an interview list waiting to be called.

1. How Customers Talk About Their Teeth

1.1 The dentist’s verdict is the proof

The result customers describe is never the chemistry. It is the moment a dentist confirms the cavity is gone. Pedro tells the full arc, from sentence to reprieve.

“The dentist said you have cavities, a big one and they said you have some more cavities in the back and you need to take out the teeth. After 3 or 4 weeks I went to the dentist and she [the dentist] didn’t find anything.”1 Persona: Frustrated repeat sufferer facing extraction or major work who wants dentist-verified proof. Stage: Use and Succeed.

The same structure repeats across Reverse Cavity’s own surfaces. Daniel describes the exam, not the product.

“He [the dentist] opened up my mouth, checked everywhere, did the test and said, I don’t see any cavities.”2 Persona: Frustrated repeat sufferer who wants dentist-verified proof. Stage: Use and Succeed.
“I used your products sometime ago and my cavity did disappear, I want to thank you for that.”2 Persona: Frustrated repeat sufferer who wants dentist-verified proof. Stage: Use and Succeed.

This pattern is the load-bearing one on the company’s own brand sites, and it is what the founder’s published case rests on too: an independent dentist reviewing his X-rays said “There is definite remineralization in these second x-rays.”10 The verdict-from-a-dentist frame is the strongest asset Reverse Cavity owns in customer language, and it appears almost nowhere else in the category, because rivals lead with credentials and mechanism instead.

1.2 Customers borrow the brand’s own word

Outside Reverse Cavity, in the open hydroxyapatite market, buyers use the exact verb the brand is named for. A Boka reviewer:

“i don’t think anything else can be said other than this toothpaste literally reversed two cavities i was developing. that’s it, that’s the review.”5 Persona: Results-driven hydroxyapatite buyer who frames success as reversed cavities. Stage: Use and Succeed.

This appears across the category, not just on Reverse Cavity’s pages. The word “reverse” is already in customers’ mouths for the whole hydroxyapatite and remineralization space, which is both the brand’s advantage and its exposure: the claim converts, and it is the same claim a regulator reads as a disease cure.

1.3 Stronger teeth and fading sensitivity are the felt signals

When customers describe the product working, they reach for two body-level signals: teeth that feel stronger, and sensitivity that fades. Nathaniel timed the strength.

“I can’t speak highly enough about Gabe’s protocol. I had been researching myself about ways to care for teeth naturally and having come across similar material, I knew he was on to something and ordered the kit! As I had some serious tooth damage at the start of the protocol… about ten days in of strictly following it there was an overall increased feeling of strength in my teeth.”3 Persona: Self-directed natural-health researcher who pre-qualified the founder before buying. Stage: Research.

N Madden tracked sensitivity instead of cavities:

“Depending on how bad the sensitivity was I used the mouthwash to help take the sensitivity away.”4 Persona: Buyer chasing functional symptom relief, not ideology. Stage: Use and Succeed.

Roland W., a Living Well customer, used the most physical language of all:

“Before I found Living wells tooth paste, my teeth were falling apart! Since using the powder and the cream style tooth paste Now my teeth are sooo much Stronger.”11 Persona: Sufferer at a breaking point seeking strength and repair. Stage: Use and Succeed.

“Stronger” and “sensitivity gone” are category-wide success words. They show up on Reverse Cavity, Boka, and Living Well alike, which means a copywriter can lead with felt strength and stay true to how every segment talks.

1.4 Distrust of dentistry is the entry point

A core segment arrives already at war with dentistry, and speaks in the language of worldview. Thekla McDaniels states it directly.

“the bigger part is that I personally don’t believe in the whole conventional dentistry story.”7 Persona: Ideological dentist-distruster who rejects the entire conventional narrative. Stage: Research.

For these buyers, the dentist is a cost center and a threat, summed up in the cattle image:

“The dentist wants to do brave and heroic dentistry, ending up with a crown, and possibly a root canal… ‘Maybe even an implant if necessary’ (an implant costs as much as a decent used car)…. Mooo, maybe I am now a cash cow for them!”7 Persona: Cost-and-distrust-driven sufferer who sees dentistry as a money grab. Stage: Awareness.

This is the founder’s intended customer, and the public record confirms the segment exists and buys on this worldview. It is also a minority of the broader category’s buyers, which Section 2 addresses head-on.

2. The Emotional Undercurrent

Four emotions run under the quotes, and they do not point the same direction.

  • Fear, edging into resignation. The dentist-avoider acts on dread that the damage is already done. A Boka buyer:
“Been avoiding the dentist lately, so I bought Boka when I was afraid all my of teeth were ruined. … My teeth feel great after a few months of using it.”6 Persona: Dentist-avoider acting on fear that teeth are already ruined. Stage: Awareness.

Hope shading into faith. A Zellie’s reviewer is not buying a mechanism, he is placing a bet on relief he has stopped expecting.

“Like the mints just hope and pray it helps my oral health and pockets in gums.”9 Persona: Hopeful, faith-tinged sufferer betting on a natural product working. Stage: Purchase.

Vindication. The dentist-avoider who gets a clean checkup feels proven right. A Permies member relayed one such report.

“I went for a cleaning and checkup after my root canal and they were shocked I hadn’t had been to a dentist in 30 years. They said it looked like I’d had regular cleanings. My dentist said to just keep doing whatever I’m doing, but if I wanted to do something extra, recommended a hydroxyapatite toothpaste.”8 Persona: Long-term dentist-avoider validated by a keep-doing-what-you’re-doing checkup. Stage: Use and Succeed.

Regret. The burned ex-believer is the emotion the brand sites never show, and the one prospects find when they search.

“I had an open tooth that I thought I’d try remineralizing. It allowed an infection to the root of the tooth. I did not want a root canal. I made it so it was a root canal or take out the tooth… I wish I had crowned it right away and had not listened to the remineralization hype.”7 Persona: Burned ex-believer who tried remineralization and regrets delaying real treatment. Stage: Use and Succeed.

2.1 The buyer-and-user split that drives messaging

Two different people buy in this category, and they want close to opposite things, so a single message cannot serve both.

The first is the founder’s ICP: frustrated, dentist-distrusting, moving from fear to hopelessness, natural-health by conviction. This buyer reads the dentist as an adversary and buys the protocol as an act of defiance and self-rescue. The cattle quote7 and the dentist-avoider’s fear6 are this person.

Mainstream buyers fill the second group all over the public reviews: people who trust their dentist, came for taste, convenience, or curiosity, and defer to clinical authority the moment it speaks. Olivia B. bought for the family and the flavor:

“I’m impressed as to what Xylitol can do to my teeth, and everyone else in my family! We have noticed an improvement and we are just starting. The best part is that these mints and gums are very tasty and we enjoy after our meals.”12 Persona: Mainstream family buyer motivated by taste, convenience, and mild curiosity, not fear. Stage: Use and Succeed.

Rachel L. went the other way on the strength of her dentist’s word:

“After using and doing more research from my dentist I found that Flouride is actually quite good for my teeth so I won’t be repurchasing.”13 Persona: Curiosity-driven crossover shopper who defers to her dentist. Stage: Evaluation.

The economic buyer in this category is often anti-dentist by conviction; the broader prospect pool trusts dentists and will defect to one mid-purchase. The same dentist who is the villain in Section 1.4 is the trusted authority for the mainstream buyer. That split is the central messaging problem this report hands the MMG: the dentist’s verdict (Section 1.1) is the bridge, because it satisfies both. The distruster reads it as vindication that even a dentist agreed; the truster reads it as permission.

3. Why They Hired: The Job Timeline

The path from first thought to repeat purchase runs through five stages, and the customer’s own words mark each one.

  • First thought, born of a diagnosis or a fear. It starts with bad news from a dentist, or the fear of getting it. Daniel’s job began with a sentence of over twenty cavities. Ryn B. did not even wait for the diagnosis: “Been avoiding the dentist lately, so I bought Boka when I was afraid all my of teeth were ruined.”6
  • Passive looking, framed as not believing the story. The distruster drifts here for a while, reading and doubting. “the bigger part is that I personally don’t believe in the whole conventional dentistry story.”7
  • Active search, pre-qualifying the source. When they commit to looking, they vet the person behind the protocol before the product. Nathaniel: “I had been researching myself about ways to care for teeth naturally and having come across similar material, I knew he was on to something and ordered the kit!”3
  • Decision, made on hope and the price of the alternative. The purchase is a bet against the used-car implant7 and a prayer that it works9.
  • First use and habit, measured in felt change. Success is logged in days and in body signals: strength at ten days3, sensitivity managed day to day4, and at the far end, a checkup that confirms decades of self-care8. The customers who reach habit are the ones who got an early felt signal, which means the protocol’s first two weeks carry the whole retention story.

4. Why They Almost Didn’t

The forces that stall or kill the sale are visible in the same public record, and three of the four are strong.

  1. Push (what drives them from the status quo). The pain of a diagnosis and the cost of the fix push hard. The implant-as-used-car line7 is the push in its purest form: the alternative is unaffordable and feels predatory.
  2. Pull (what attracts them to the new way). The pull is a felt, fast result they can verify themselves, and ideally a dentist who confirms it1.
  3. Anxiety (what scares them about switching). This is the strongest brake, and it is well-founded. Prospects who search find the regret. The open-tooth story7 is the anxiety made concrete: delay natural treatment, lose the tooth. Willy H. names a softer version, the fear of being fooled:
“So I’ve been buying this product for 3 years now because the exposure on social media and how good it’s supposed to be for your teeth. However, come to find out, it’s actually not that great, not effective and the ingredients are not as good as they appear.”14 Persona: Social-media-led buyer who later feels misled about natural efficacy claims. Stage: Use and Succeed.

Habit (what keeps them where they are). For the mainstream buyer, the deepest habit is trusting the dentist, and it pulls them back. Rachel L. returned to fluoride on her dentist’s advice13. Another Permies member ran the natural protocol for two years, saw nothing, and went back to the chair:

“I have a very large cavity in one of my molars. I tried to oil pull, use a diy remineralizing toothpaste (bentonite clay, calcium/magnesium, baking soda), and eat as clean as possible for about 2 years. I couldn’t keep it up, and didn’t see any difference after my experimentation. I finally got the cavity filled this past year.”7 Persona: Earnest natural-remedy trier who gave up after no results. Stage: Use and Succeed.

Anxiety and habit win a meaningful share of these sales for the dentist. The defense is the same dentist-confirmation proof that wins the believers, plus an honest boundary about what the protocol can and cannot do.

5. The Language Gap

Customers and vendors describe the same product in different vocabularies. The vendor column below is the category cliche, the language the whole remineralization and hydroxyapatite space leans on, not any one company’s copy.

What customers say (verbatim) What vendors say (category cliche) The shift to make
“After 3 or 4 weeks I went to the dentist and she [the dentist] didn’t find anything.”1 Clinically formulated to remineralize enamel Lead with the dentist’s confirmation visit, not the mechanism.
“literally reversed two cavities i was developing”5 Supports your body’s natural remineralization process Use the customer’s blunt verb, inside a defensible boundary about early decay.
“an overall increased feeling of strength in my teeth”3 Strengthens and protects enamel Promise a felt change with a timeframe customers report, around ten days.
“maybe I am now a cash cow for them!”7 An affordable alternative to dental care Name the money fear directly: the used-car implant, the cash-cow visit.
“hope and pray it helps my oral health”9 Trusted by thousands Meet the hope with proof, not with social-proof volume.

Words to adopt: reverse, the dentist didn’t find anything, stronger, feel it in days, no drilling, no extraction, see for yourself at your next checkup.

Words to drop: remineralize as the lead word, inside-out mechanism, clinically formulated, optimize, support your body’s natural process, holistic wellness. These are the category’s words, and customers neither use them nor are moved by them.

6. What Customers Feel But Do Not Say

Three things sit under the quotes without being stated outright, each read from patterns the quotes themselves support.

  1. They are afraid they have already waited too long. Ryn B. bought when she was afraid all her teeth were ruined6 and the regretful ex-believer made it so it was a root canal or losing the tooth7. The unspoken question on the way in is whether it is already too late, which is why an early felt signal matters so much to retention.
  2. They are quietly bracing to be told they were foolish. The distrust of dentistry is loud; the fear of being judged by one is silent. The vindication in the 30-year-checkup story8 only lands because the customer expected to be scolded and was not. Messaging that respects the buyer’s intelligence, and does not mock dentists, defuses the unspoken shame.
  3. They do not fully trust the brand’s own glowing testimonials. Willy H. bought on three years of social-media hype and felt misled14. The unvoiced complaint is that brand-curated raves read as marketing. The dentist’s independent verdict is the one form of proof that escapes this doubt.

7. Rave Notes: A Testimonial Bank

These are the strongest advocacy verbatims, grouped by the claim a copywriter would build on. Each carries its source citation.

7.1 The dentist confirmed it

“The dentist said you have cavities, a big one and they said you have some more cavities in the back and you need to take out the teeth. After 3 or 4 weeks I went to the dentist and she [the dentist] didn’t find anything.”1 Persona: Frustrated repeat sufferer. Stage: Use and Succeed.
“He [the dentist] opened up my mouth, checked everywhere, did the test and said, I don’t see any cavities.”2 Persona: Frustrated repeat sufferer. Stage: Use and Succeed.
“I used your products sometime ago and my cavity did disappear, I want to thank you for that.”2 Persona: Quality-sensitive buyer. Stage: Use and Succeed.
“There is definite remineralization in these second x-rays.”10 Persona: Independent dentist reviewing the founder’s X-rays. Stage: Use and Succeed.

7.2 I felt it work, fast

“I can’t speak highly enough about Gabe’s protocol. I had been researching myself about ways to care for teeth naturally and having come across similar material, I knew he was on to something and ordered the kit! As I had some serious tooth damage at the start of the protocol… about ten days in of strictly following it there was an overall increased feeling of strength in my teeth.”3 Persona: Self-directed natural-health researcher. Stage: Research.
“Before I found Living wells tooth paste, my teeth were falling apart! Since using the powder and the cream style tooth paste Now my teeth are sooo much Stronger.”11 Persona: Sufferer at a breaking point. Stage: Use and Succeed.
“i don’t think anything else can be said other than this toothpaste literally reversed two cavities i was developing. that’s it, that’s the review.”5 Persona: Results-driven hydroxyapatite buyer. Stage: Use and Succeed.

7.3 It treated my child, my family, my whole system

“We used your mouthwash, to help reverse my son’s cavities.”15 Persona: Natural-leaning parent treating a child’s cavities at home. Stage: Use and Succeed.
“I’m impressed as to what Xylitol can do to my teeth, and everyone else in my family! We have noticed an improvement and we are just starting. The best part is that these mints and gums are very tasty and we enjoy after our meals.”12 Persona: Mainstream family buyer. Stage: Use and Succeed.
“Before I was always tired, but I lost weight, I feel a lot of energy. The energy level improved, it is really good. My circle of sleeping is more full. During the day I have more energy… No heaviness in my stomach at all. When I take the powder my sleeping is really good.”16 Persona: Whole-body wellness buyer. Stage: Use and Succeed.

8. Strategic Implications

Each implication translates what customers say into a move the company can act on. The first is the precondition for trusting all the rest.

Commission a primary-interview and case-study program (AI-led custom interviews). This report runs on public reviews because no Reverse Cavity customer was interviewed. The founder already holds roughly two dozen written testimonials and seven video ones, an interview list waiting to be called. Interview them and a set of lapsed buyers to confirm the emotional registers, the buyer-and-user split, the job timeline, and the win-loss forces named above, then feed the verbatim language into the MMG. Commission it.

  1. Make the dentist’s confirmation visit the lead proof. Customers describe success as the dentist finding nothing1. They never describe it as remineralization. The independent-dentist confirmation of the founder’s own X-rays10 is the rare proof that converts the distruster and reassures the truster at once. This reinforces the Brief’s call to re-anchor the brand on documented, dentist-confirmed proof, the one claim rivals leading with the inside-out mechanism cannot match. Lead with it.
  2. Segment the message to the buyer-and-user split. The anti-dentist believer7 and the dentist-trusting mainstream buyer13 want close to opposite things, and one message will not hold both. Build the MMG’s audience layer around two registers, with the dentist-confirmation proof as the shared bridge. Segment it.
  3. Set an honest claim boundary that survives the skeptics. Prospects who search find the regret7 and the feeling of being misled14 before they find a brand testimonial. An unqualified reverse-your-cavities-in-3-months headline reads to a regulator as a disease cure and to a burned ex-user as the hype that cost them a tooth. Sharpen the public claim to arresting and remineralizing early decay and strengthening enamel, matching the Brief’s claim-ceiling recommendation. Rewrite it.
  4. Treat the public review surface as a sales floor and manage it. Buyers in this category make the decision while reading outside reviews mid-evaluation, where Boka’s defections14, Rachel’s return to fluoride13, and the open-tooth regret7 live alongside the raves. The review pages on Thingtesting, Permies, Zellie’s, and Living Well are a sales channel the company does not yet manage. Seed and steward Reverse Cavity’s own dentist-confirmation reviews where prospects actually read, and respond to the regret stories with the honest boundary above. Manage it.

Sources

  • Reverse Cavity site (reverseautoimmune.com), Reverse Cavity Protocol Kit landing page testimonial, by Pedro, n.d. (https://www.reverseautoimmune.com/reverse-cavity-protocol-kit-landing-page/)
  • Reverse Cavity homepage testimonial block (reversecavity.com), ‘Does it work?’ section, by Daniel C and Dennis C (https://reversecavity.com/)
  • Reverse Cavity site (reverseautoimmune.com), Reverse Cavity Protocol Kit landing page testimonial, by Nathaniel, n.d. (https://www.reverseautoimmune.com/reverse-cavity-protocol-kit-landing-page/)
  • Reverse Cavity site (reverseautoimmune.com), Reverse Cavity Protocol Kit landing page testimonial, by N Madden, n.d. (https://www.reverseautoimmune.com/reverse-cavity-protocol-kit-landing-page/)
  • Thingtesting, Boka reviews, by spoiled < 3, ~3y ago (https://thingtesting.com/brands/boka/reviews)
  • Thingtesting, Boka reviews, by Ryn B, ~3y ago (https://thingtesting.com/brands/boka/reviews)
  • Permies forum, ‘Tooth remineralization quandary’ thread, by Thekla McDaniels and other members (https://permies.com/t/202894/personal-care/Tooth-remineralization-quandary)
  • Permies forum, ‘Tooth remineralization quandary’ thread, by Jan White, relayed by another member (https://permies.com/t/202894/personal-care/Tooth-remineralization-quandary)
  • Zellie’s (shop.zellies.com), Cool Mint Xylitol Dental Mints review, by Stephen H., n.d. (https://shop.zellies.com/pages/reviews)
  • Life & Health Network, ‘How I Reversed 5 Cavities in 3 Months,’ by Gabriel Arruda, Mar 4 2026, independent dentist quoted reviewing the founder’s X-rays (https://lifeandhealth.org/lifestyle/healthy-habits/how-i-reversed-5-cavities-in-3-months/1023384.html)
  • Living Well with Dr. Michelle Jorgensen (livingwellwithdrmichelle.com), Remineralizing Toothpaste 1 Year Supply, verified customer review by Roland W., n.d. (https://livingwellwithdrmichelle.com/products/remineralizing-toothpaste-1-year-supply)
  • Zellie’s (shop.zellies.com), Mint Bulk Bag review, by Olivia B., n.d. (https://shop.zellies.com/pages/reviews)
  • Thingtesting, Boka reviews, by Rachel L, ~9mo ago (https://thingtesting.com/brands/boka/reviews)
  • Thingtesting, Boka reviews, by Willy H, ~1y ago (https://thingtesting.com/brands/boka/reviews)
